{"step_by_step_thinking": "In cardiac tamponade, pulsus paradoxus is a key clinical sign, defined as a drop in systolic blood pressure of more than 10 mm Hg during inspiration. This occurs due to the collapse of the right heart during inspiration, which reduces venous return and cardiac output. To accurately assess blood pressure and detect this paradoxical drop, the patient must breathe normally. Holding the breath (option A) or taking deep or shallow breaths (options C and D) would interfere with the natural respiratory cycle and could mask or distort the pulsus paradoxus. The standard of care, as supported by multiple documents, emphasizes measuring blood pressure during both inspiration and expiration to detect this drop. Therefore, the correct advice is to breathe normally to ensure accurate and reliable measurements. Option B is consistent with clinical guidelines and best practices.", "answer_choice": "B"}
